info.title: Kissflow API documentation
info.description:
# Getting started with Kissflow APIs

Kissflow APIs let you remotely manage users, groups, workflows, dataforms, datasets, portals, and reports within the Kissflow platform: create, read, update, and delete these resources programmatically.

Kissflow APIs are RESTful web services. This documentation describes every API endpoint in detail.

# API essentials

We recommend familiarity with the following:

**Programming knowledge**: Basic proficiency in a language such as JavaScript, Python, Ruby, or Java, depending on the API's technology stack.

**HTTP and REST principles**: Familiarity with HTTP methods and RESTful principles.

| HTTP method | Description |
| --- | --- |
| GET | Retrieves data from a resource. |
| POST | Creates a resource or initiates an action. |
| PUT | Updates a resource. |
| DELETE | Deletes a resource. |

**JSON data format**: Ability to generate, parse, and manipulate JSON, the data-exchange format used across Kissflow APIs.

**Authentication and security**: Knowledge of authentication methods (OAuth, API keys, basic authentication) and best practices for securing API endpoints.

**Tools and libraries**: Familiarity with tools for making HTTP requests, such as cURL, and handling API responses.

**Error handling and debugging**: Ability to troubleshoot API interactions, handle errors, and debug issues during integration.

# Fundamental data types

These are the fundamental data types and formats used across API parameters, including header, path, query, and request body parameters.

| Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| String | A sequence of characters with Unicode support. |
| Integer | A signed 64-bit integral number. |
| Boolean | Represents a value of either **true** or **false**. |
| Date | A date formatted in ISO 8601 extended format: YYYY-MM-DD. |
| DateTime | Combined date and time formatted in ISO 8601 extended format: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS±hh:mm. For example, 2018-12-03T10:15:30+01:00 (Europe/Paris). |

# Base URL

The base URL is the foundation for every endpoint URL. It's defined by a protocol, host, and base path:

```
{protocol}://{host}/{basePath}
```

For example:

```
https://demo.kissflow.com/process/2
```

1. **Protocol**: HTTP or HTTPS.
2. **Host**: A subdomain and domain name. The subdomain is unique to each Kissflow account; the domain is kissflow.com or kissflow.eu.
3. **Base path**: The service name and API version string. All Kissflow API endpoints use version 2.

## Resource URLs

API requests combine the base URL with a specific resource or collection path.

#### Example API endpoint:

```
https://{subdomain}.kissflow.{region}/{service}/2/{path_params}
```

# Testing APIs

Test APIs directly from the terminal using the cURL and HTTPie native tester tools, or send a request from the API reference UI:

1. Under the Authorization tab in the right panel, enter the **Access Key ID** and **Access Key Secret** for HTTP request header authentication (sent as the `X-Access-Key-Id` and `X-Access-Key-Secret` headers).
2. Edit the example cURL or HTTPie command with the request details.
3. Click **Send**.

## Rate limiting

> **Note:** To distribute resources fairly among API users and prevent abuse, Kissflow enforces a limit of 400 API calls per minute. This limit includes both programmatic and UI-based invocations.

### API payload size limits

The Kissflow API enforces a maximum payload size of 50 MB per request. Payloads that exceed this limit return an error.

> **Note**: This limit applies to every API endpoint that accepts a request payload.

## Security system limitation

Security systems may block some requests before they reach Kissflow services, causing intermittent connectivity issues or failed API requests. [Learn more](https://community.kissflow.com/t/g9yggqv).

# HTTP request content type

The Content-Type header specifies the format of the data sent to the server. Kissflow supports the following content types for POST and PUT requests.

| Content-Type | Description | Usage |
| --- | --- | --- |
| application/json | Indicates that the request body contains JSON data. | Used widely for RESTful APIs because JSON is lightweight and easy to read. |
| multipart/form-data | Used for sending files and data in a form. | Used for file uploads or when files and form data need to be sent together. |

# HTTP response content type

The Content-Type header in a response indicates the format of the returned content. Kissflow API responses are in JSON format.

| Content-Type | Description | Usage |
| --- | --- | --- |
| application/json | Indicates that the response body contains JSON data. | Used widely for RESTful APIs because JSON is lightweight, readable, and broadly supported. |

# API authentication

Kissflow requires an **Access Key ID** and **Access Key Secret** to access protected resources, sent as the `X-Access-Key-Id` and `X-Access-Key-Secret` headers. [Follow these instructions](https://community.kissflow.com/t/35h4az8/api-authentication) to create an Access Key ID and Access Key Secret in a Kissflow account.

**Note**:

- Kissflow generates the Access Key ID and Access Key Secret at the individual user level, not the account level.
- Users can create multiple access keys within a single account.
- Delete unused access keys to maintain security.
- Access keys authenticate identity and must be kept private to prevent misuse.

> **Warning:** Make API calls from the web application server as a best practice. Avoid direct browser calls using JavaScript, which expose API keys. Use HTTPS/TLS/SSL with basic authentication, since base64 encoding is reversible and not secure.

# HTTP response status codes

HTTP response status codes indicate whether the server completed a request. Responses fall into five classes: informational (100–199), success (200–299), redirection (300–399), client error (400–499), and server error (500–599).

Common response status codes:

| HTTP status code | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 200 | Success | The server processed the request successfully. |
| 201 | Created | The request succeeded and created a new resource. Typically returned after POST requests or some PUT requests. |
| 400 | Bad request | The server can't process the request due to a client error, such as malformed syntax, invalid request framing, or deceptive request routing. |
| 401 | Unauthorized | The client attempted to access a resource that requires authentication, but the provided credentials are invalid, missing, or not accepted by the server. |
| 403 | Forbidden | The client requested a resource it isn't authorized to access, even though the resource exists. The server understood the request but refuses to fulfill it due to permission restrictions or authentication issues. **Note**: If the response header includes `cf-mitigated: challenge`, security checks blocked the request before it reached Kissflow services. |
| 404 | Resource not found | The server can't find the requested resource. In a browser, this means the URL isn't recognized. In an API, it can also mean the endpoint is valid but the resource doesn't exist. Servers may also return this instead of 403 Forbidden to hide a resource's existence from an unauthorized client. |
| 500 | Internal server error | Something went wrong on the server, but the server can't specify the exact problem. This catch-all response covers unexpected conditions the server encounters while processing a request. |
| 501 | Not implemented | The server doesn't support the request method and can't handle it. Servers must support GET and HEAD, so this code never applies to them. |
| 502 | Bad gateway | The server, acting as a gateway or proxy, received an invalid response from the upstream server it sent the request to. |
| 503 | Service unavailable | The server isn't ready to handle the request, typically due to maintenance or overload. The response includes a user-friendly explanation, and a Retry-After header may indicate the estimated recovery time. |
| 504 | Gateway timeout | The server, acting as a gateway, didn't receive a response in time. |

subdomain.description: Replace with your Kissflow account subdomain — e.g. "acme" for acme.kissflow.com. The default is a placeholder; try-it returns a 403 DomainMissMatchError until you set your real account subdomain (and fill any path params + Access Key auth).

tags.name: Users and groups
tags.description: Manages users and groups, controlling access and permissions across a Kissflow account.

tags.name: Users and groups > Users
tags.description: Creates user accounts with profile information and access rights, retrieves user lists with filtering and sorting, retrieves individual user details, and updates multiple user accounts in bulk.

tags.name: Users and groups > Groups
tags.description: Creates and deletes user groups, retrieves group lists and details including members, and adds or removes users from a group.

tags.name: Portals
tags.description: Portals are applications that let external users, such as vendors, customers, or partners, take part in business operations without an account in the Kissflow account. Portals support the same [process](https://api.kissflow.com/#bfb27b53-f18a-4fd4-93ed-7aaa73ce1b2f), [dataform](https://api.kissflow.com/#9f791d8b-1a77-47cc-86b5-67d05289d138), and [board](https://api.kissflow.com/#33a95d68-0efc-4c70-8c2d-cf30c8a61ce0) API endpoints as the main account. Use the [portal domain](https://community.kissflow.com/t/p8h4atv/administering-portals#portal-details) as the subdomain in the endpoint URL, with an [access key](https://community.kissflow.com/t/q6h4akr/creating-and-managing-access-keys) generated from the portal's service account.

tags.name: Processes
tags.description: A process is a structured workflow that moves a task through a defined, sequential order. Process endpoints are also available within [portals](https://api.kissflow.com/#587a3da2-9417-440d-8322-1322ec870a32); see the Portals section for the subdomain and access key requirements.

tags.name: Boards
tags.description: A board is a customizable workflow for tracking information and managing work. Board endpoints are also available within [portals](https://api.kissflow.com/#587a3da2-9417-440d-8322-1322ec870a32); see the Portals section for the subdomain and access key requirements.

tags.name: Dataforms and dataform views
tags.description: A dataform is a data-collection form that stores submitted data without a workflow approval process. Dataform endpoints are also available within [portals](https://api.kissflow.com/#587a3da2-9417-440d-8322-1322ec870a32); see the Portals section for the subdomain and access key requirements.

tags.name: Dataforms and dataform views > Dataforms
tags.description: A dataform is a data-collection form that stores submitted data without a workflow approval process.

tags.name: Dataforms and dataform views > Dataform views
tags.description: A dataform view is a customized perspective on a dataform's data, built with filters, sorting, and calculations applied without altering the underlying dataform.

tags.name: Dataset and dataset views
tags.description: A dataset is a structured collection of data, imported from external sources or generated within Kissflow. A dataset view is a customized perspective on a dataset, built with filters, sorting, and calculations to present specific subsets of data without modifying the original dataset.

post.summary: Bulk create or update dataform records
post.description:
Creates or updates up to 500 records in a dataform in a single request. Send a JSON array in the request body, where each object represents one record operation.

To update an existing record, identify it by its unique `_id` string and omit the `_is_created` field. Example: `{ "_id": "existing-record-123", "fieldName": "new value", "anotherField": "updated data" }`.

To create a new record, provide all required fields (and any optional fields) and set `_is_created` to `true`. Example: `{ "_is_created": true, "requiredFieldA": "value A", "requiredFieldB": 123, "optionalFieldC": "some data" }`.

A single request can mix create and update objects. Field names and data types must match the dataform's schema.

schema.description: A list of record operations, each either an update (identified by _id) or a create (marked with _is_created: true). Up to 500 records can be created or updated per request. The exact fields depend on your dataform.

items.description: A single create or update operation. To update: include _id and the fields to change. To create: include _is_created: true and the new record's field values. Because dataform fields are account-specific, only the operation-marker semantics (_id for update, _is_created for create) are guaranteed; other fields are not marked required here.

_id.description: Identifier of the existing record to update. Required to update a record; must be omitted when creating a new record.

_is_created.description: Set to true to create a new record instead of updating an existing one. Must be omitted (not present) when updating.

TotalPrice.description: A dataform field. The exact fields depend on your form.

PONumber.description: A dataform field. The exact fields depend on your form.

Name.description: A dataform field. The exact fields depend on your form.

RequestedBy.description: A dataform lookup-field value. The exact fields depend on your form.

_id.description: Unique identifier of the referenced record/user.

Name.description: Display name of the referenced record/user.

schema.description: The records that were created or updated by this bulk operation. Each item includes whichever fields were included in that record's create or update operation, plus the system fields _id and _modified_at.

items.description: A single record after the create/update. Field names beyond the system fields (_id, _modified_at) are dataform-specific and vary per item based on what was submitted for that record; only _id and _modified_at are common to every item in this example.

parameters.description: The Kissflow account ID; see how to retrieve one from the [Account ID](https://community.kissflow.com/t/35h4az8/api-authentication#account-id) guide.

parameters.name: dataset_id
parameters.description: The unique ID of the dataset, taken from the dataset URL — for example, `Bank_Dataset` in `https://thestarkcorporation.kissflow.com/view/dataset/Bank_Dataset`.

parameters.name: row_id
parameters.description: The unique ID of the row.

parameters.name: field_id
parameters.description: The unique ID of the field.

schema.description: Wrapper containing the dataset record(s) affected by the image upload.

response.description: Dataset record(s) reflecting the newly attached image.
